output:
# ISOWASH and STRATA: Depth-Conditional Style Strength for Multi-View-Consistent 3D Style Transfer of Neural Radiance Fields

A high-level summarized insight of a research field (Machine Learning / 3D vision):
artistic style transfer for Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) supervises a source radiance
field with a 2D style objective -- in the source Introduction, a Sliced Wasserstein
Distance (SWD) loss against style images prepared in advance by a depth-conditioned,
text-guided style-aligned diffusion model. This document analyzes that pipeline, identifies
a precise representational blind spot in how it applies style strength, and proposes a new
research idea in the required five-question (5q) format. The proposal applies a reusable
research spine -- promote a single global scalar to a structured, monotone, partially-pooled
map of an observable covariate, with a provable exact-collapse identity back to the original
method -- that has been instantiated across many machine-learning subfields in this team's
prior work. Here the promoted scalar is the global style strength of an SWD-supervised NeRF,
and the observable covariate is the radiance field's own rendered depth.

Notation is kept ASCII throughout: "lambda" and "theta" and "psi" are scalars/parameters,
"z" is depth, "zhat" is normalized depth, "<=" and ">=" are inequalities, "->" is an arrow,
"approx" is approximately-equal, "sum_r" is a sum over rays r, and "x" denotes multiplication.

---

## Section 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 Implicit 3D representation and the nerfacto backbone

Neural Radiance Fields [1] represent a scene as a continuous volumetric function mapping a
3D position and view direction to color and density, rendered by differentiable volume
integration along camera rays. NeRF produces photo-realistic novel views with a compact
model and, importantly for this proposal, renders a per-ray expected depth essentially for
free as a by-product of the same volumetric integration. The source Introduction builds on
the "nerfacto" model in Nerfstudio [2], a modular combination of proposal sampling and
scene contraction from Mip-NeRF 360 [3], the multiresolution hash encoding of Instant-NGP
[4], the appearance/transient handling of NeRF-in-the-Wild [5], and the view-dependent
appearance structure of Ref-NeRF [6], chosen to balance speed and quality. The same
volumetric depth signal exists in faster point-based fields such as 3D Gaussian Splatting
[7], so any method that consumes rendered depth transfers across backbones. Standard capture
and benchmark scenes used by this line include Local Light Field Fusion forward-facing
captures [8] and the unbounded outdoor scenes of Mip-NeRF 360 [3].

### 1.2 3D style transfer and 3D editing

Editing a reconstructed 3D scene to match a desired style is far harder than reconstructing
it. Two families dominate. The first leverages large pre-trained 2D text-to-image (T2I)
models to supervise NeRF editing: Instruct-NeRF2NeRF [9] iteratively edits and replaces the
NeRF's own training images with InstructPix2Pix [16] edits during optimization, and
ViCA-NeRF [10] adds explicit cross-view correspondence to reduce inconsistency. As the
source Introduction notes, because the 2D editor runs in tandem with NeRF training, these
methods involve an iterative edit-and-replace loop that "occasionally results in
unpredictable results" and makes it hard to test a style before committing to the full NeRF
optimization. The second family transfers artistic style into the radiance field directly:
ARF [11] matches deep features of the rendered views to a style image with a nearest-neighbor
feature-matching loss and deferred back-propagation; StyleRF [12] performs zero-shot
feature-space transfer in the radiance field; SNeRF [13] alternates 2D stylization and NeRF
optimization; NeRF-Art [14] and the CLIP [25] / CLIPstyler [26] line drive stylization with
text; and Ref-NPR [15] propagates a stylized reference view into a controllable
non-photorealistic field. The source Introduction sits in this second family (referred to below
as the source method): it prepares stylized images in advance with a depth-conditioned [18]
style-aligned [17] diffusion model on a latent-diffusion backbone [19] and then fine-tunes the
source NeRF (nerfacto [2]) on them with an SWD loss [20], so a user can test stylization ideas
in the diffusion stage before paying for NeRF fine-tuning.

### 1.3 Style-aligned diffusion and depth conditioning

The source pipeline's front end is a text-guided diffusion model whose generations are made
mutually consistent in appearance via style-aligned generation [17] and are conditioned on
the depth maps of the corresponding source views via a structural-control adapter in the
ControlNet [18] family, on top of a latent diffusion backbone [19]. The crucial observation
for this proposal is that depth is already a first-class signal in the pipeline: it is
rendered by the NeRF and consumed by the diffusion model as a structural control. Depth is
therefore observable, already-computed, and leakage-free with respect to the style objective.

### 1.4 The SWD style objective and 2D style transfer

Style transfer originates with Gatys et al. [22], who matched Gram statistics of deep VGG
features, and was made fast and arbitrary by AdaIN [23] and WCT [24]. The source method's
supervisory signal is the Sliced Wasserstein Distance loss for neural texture synthesis of
Heitz et al. [20] (with long-range extensions [21]): it matches the full distribution of
deep features between a rendered image and a style image by projecting features onto many
random 1D directions and matching the sorted projections, a stronger statistical match than
Gram matrices. Two facts about this objective matter here. First, it operates at a fixed
image-space feature scale (the receptive field of the chosen VGG layers and the resolution
at which slices are taken). Second, as used, it applies one global style strength uniformly:
every pixel/ray contributes to the matched feature distribution with the same weight,
regardless of where the corresponding surface sits in 3D. A separate, smaller 2D literature
already noticed that depth should modulate stylization: depth-aware neural style transfer
[27, 28] preserves content layout and, in the case of Kitov et al. [27], explicitly varies
stylization strength by estimated depth -- but strictly within single 2D images, with a
heuristic depth-loss/strength schedule, no radiance field, and no notion of multi-view 3D
consistency.

### 1.5 The reusable analytical lens (prior team memory)

Across many prior machine-learning subfields, this team has repeatedly found the same
failure shape: a strong method hard-codes a single global scalar -- a mixup rate, a learning
-rate bound, an image-partition split, a divergence choice, a safety-gating threshold, a
quantum-kernel bandwidth -- applied identically to every instance, blind to an observable
per-instance covariate. Because a constant function cannot equal a non-constant per-instance
optimum, the scalar is simultaneously too large for some instances and too small for others;
the two-sided error cancels in any pooled average, so the harm is invisible under the field's
headline metric, grows with instance heterogeneity, and vanishes when instances are
homogeneous. The fix is always the same shape: replace the scalar with a structured,
monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map of the observable covariate, fit with empirical
-Bayes shrinkage [30, 31] so data-poor regions fall back to the global default, and prove an
exact-collapse identity so the new method reduces to the original at its default setting and
can be shadow-deployed with no regression at the default. This document is the next instantiation of
that lens, applied for the first time to 3D style transfer of radiance fields.

### 1.6 The blind spot in SWD-supervised NeRF stylization

The source method's headline setting is large-scale outdoor 360 scenes, which by definition
have an enormous depth range (near foreground to far background/sky). Yet its style strength
is a single global value applied to the whole field, blind to depth. Section 2 formalizes
why this is a representational impossibility and, more importantly, why depth-blindness is
not merely a tuning nuisance but a representational contributor to the multi-view inconsistency
("flicker", "unpredictable results") that this entire line of work struggles with.

---

## Section 2. Key Findings and Analysis

### 2.1 The gap, named: ISOWASH (Isotropic stylization-Wash)

Define the source fine-tuning objective schematically as a style-supervision loss over camera
rays r,

    L_base = sum_r lambda x ell_style(r) + (content/regularization terms),

where ell_style(r) is ray r's contribution to the style match (the per-ray term against the
prepared stylized image, together with the SWD distribution-matching term computed over the
rendered features), and lambda is a single global style strength applied identically to every
ray. ISOWASH is the property that lambda is a depth-blind global constant: the same artistic
"wash" is applied at every 3D distance. Let z_r be ray r's rendered depth (the NeRF's expected
ray-termination distance) and zhat_r in [0,1] its normalized value. The depth-optimal style
strength lambda*(zhat) is in general a non-constant function of depth for two independent,
physically grounded reasons:

(a) Aerial perspective. In real scenes and in painterly practice, distant surfaces are hazier,
lower in contrast, and carry less high-frequency texture than near surfaces. A correct
stylization should therefore deposit less fine stylistic detail at large depth and more at
small depth; a single global lambda cannot express this graded falloff.

(b) Perspective foreshortening and multi-view consistency (a deeper contributor). The SWD/VGG
objective imposes texture at a fixed image-space scale s_img. But a fixed image-space texture
corresponds to a world-space texture scale s_world approx s_img x z that VARIES with depth and
with viewpoint, because a world surface patch projects to an image footprint that shrinks like
1/z (up to focal length, surface orientation, and view angle). Concretely: the same world
surface seen from a near viewpoint (small z, large image footprint) and a far viewpoint (large
z, small image footprint) is asked, by a global image-space style objective, to carry two
DIFFERENT world-space textures. A single 3D radiance field cannot simultaneously satisfy both
demands, so optimization tends to average them -- contributing blur, floaters, and
view-dependent flicker. This is closely related to the multi-view inconsistency that
Instruct-NeRF2NeRF [9] reports as "unpredictable results" and that ViCA-NeRF [10] tries to
patch with correspondence. ISOWASH reframes that inconsistency as, in part, a representational
symptom of a depth-blind global style scale, not purely a stochastic training artifact. Two
responses follow: reduce style STRENGTH where unsupportable fine detail cannot be made
view-consistent (knob K1, which is also the aerial-perspective response), and, more directly,
compensate the style SCALE with depth so the world-space texture scale is held approximately
constant (knob K2). K1 mitigates the symptom; K2 targets the scale mechanism itself.

Representational impossibility (made clear). Because lambda*(zhat) is non-constant whenever a
scene spans a range of depths, no single scalar lambda equals lambda*(zhat) everywhere: the
best global compromise over-stylizes the far band (depositing fine texture that has the wrong
world-space scale and breaks across views) while under-stylizing the near band (leaving it
flatter than the style warrants), or vice versa. The error is two-sided across depth.

### 2.2 Why the harm is SILENT, SCALES, and VANISHES

Silent. The field evaluates a stylized NeRF with pooled, whole-image metrics: an aggregate
style-match score (SWD/Gram/CLIP-style distance averaged over the image), aggregate content
/structure preservation (LPIPS or depth error to the content field), single-view perceptual
quality, and qualitative novel-view renders. Every one of these averages over depth. The
far-band over-stylization and near-band under-stylization carry opposite signs and partially
cancel in the image mean, and the multi-view flicker they cause concentrates in specific
depth bands that the pooled, per-view scores do not isolate. At the single best global lambda,
these metrics therefore show a flat, healthy-looking optimum even though a depth-resolved view
would reveal large, structured, opposite-signed errors.

Scales. The magnitude of the unavoidable compromise error grows with the scene's depth
heterogeneity -- its depth range and the spread of rendered depth across rays. Large-scale
outdoor 360 captures [3], the source method's headline setting, are the worst case.

Vanishes. For a flat, fronto-parallel scene (all rays at essentially constant depth, zhat_r
approx z_const), the world-space and image-space scales coincide up to a global constant and
the depth-optimal strength is itself constant: lambda*(zhat) = lambda* for all rays, and a
single global lambda is optimal. The gap closes exactly when depth is homogeneous, which both
explains why 2D and near-planar style transfer never had to confront it and pins the covariate
(depth) that controls the harm.

### 2.3 The binding obstacle: metric blindness

The reason ISOWASH has gone unaddressed is not that it is subtle to fix but that it is
invisible to measure under current practice. The binding obstacle is metric blindness: pooled
whole-image style and content metrics, and per-view quality scores, average away the
depth-band-localized, opposite-signed errors and the depth-band-localized multi-view flicker.
Detecting the gap requires (i) depth-stratified evaluation -- multi-view consistency and
stylization quality reported per depth band, especially the far band -- and (ii) falsifiers
that separate a genuine depth mechanism from a relabeled global tweak (an isotropic-null
control on low-depth-range non-degenerate scenes, with exactly-flat scenes as trivial-collapse
sanity checks, a covariate-scramble placebo, and a monotone-dose test across depth
range). Without these, the source method's pooled numbers look optimal at its tuned global
lambda, which is precisely why neither it nor the surrounding 3D-style literature noticed the
degeneracy.

### 2.4 Summary of the analysis

The source method's global style strength is a degenerate, depth-blind special case of a
depth-conditional style-strength field. The degeneracy is most severe exactly where the method
is showcased (large-depth-range outdoor scenes), it is a representational source of the
field's multi-view-consistency problem, and it is hidden by pooled metrics. This motivates a
method that promotes the global scalar to an observable-depth-conditional map while provably
collapsing back to the original method at its default -- developed as a concrete research
proposal in Section 3.

---

## Section 3. Novel Research Idea (the 5q)

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.

Here are the five core questions:

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can the single global, depth-blind style strength that a Sliced-Wasserstein-supervised NeRF
uses to stylize an entire 3D scene uniformly be replaced by a per-ray, depth-conditional style
strength -- a structured, monotone, partially-pooled map of the radiance field's own rendered
depth -- that improves multi-view-consistent stylization on large-depth-range (outdoor 360)
scenes while provably collapsing to the global-strength method at its default setting?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Multi-view consistency is the central open problem of 3D style transfer and editing. The
instruction-driven line openly reports "unpredictable results" and view-dependent artifacts
[9], and follow-up work spends its novelty budget on correspondence machinery to suppress
them [10]; the direct-stylization line [11, 12, 13, 14, 15] shares the symptom. Section 2
shows that a depth-blind global style SCALE is a representational contributor to this
inconsistency, because a fixed image-space texture maps to a depth- and view-varying
world-space texture that a single 3D field cannot simultaneously satisfy across viewpoints (up
to focal length and surface orientation). A method that makes the style strength (knob K1) and,
crucially for the scale argument, the style scale (knob K2) functions of rendered depth attacks
this mechanism directly and physically -- K1 via aerial perspective, K2 via approximate
world-space-scale constancy -- rather than only patching symptoms after the fact.

The broader implications are fourfold. (1) A reusable design principle: a global stylization
hyperparameter is reframed as a function of an observable covariate (depth) that the pipeline
already computes and already feeds to its diffusion front end, turning a hand-tuned constant
into a learned, auditable map. This principle transfers immediately to any feature-space or
SWD/NNFM 3D stylizer [11, 12] and to point-based fields such as 3D Gaussian Splatting [7],
because all of them render depth. (2) A do-no-harm deployment story: because the method
collapses exactly to the host at its default, it can be layered on top of an existing
production 3D stylizer and shadow-evaluated with no regression at the exact-collapse default
(active depth-conditional settings are validation-gated), lowering the barrier to
adoption. (3) A measurement contribution: depth-stratified multi-view-consistency evaluation
is a diagnostic the field currently lacks, and it will outlive any single method by exposing
where stylization breaks in 3D. (4) A practical artist-facing win: aerial-perspective-correct,
depth-graded stylization (more fine style near, less far) is a real artistic desideratum that
global methods cannot deliver, achieved here automatically and previewable in the diffusion
stage before NeRF fine-tuning. A paper that establishes the depth-conditional reframing, the
consistency mechanism, the exact-collapse safety identity, and the depth-stratified metric
gives future 3D-editing work a new axis (per-depth style allocation) and a new yardstick.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The difficulty is technical, statistical, and methodological, and naive approaches fail on
each axis.

O1 -- Entangled per-depth optimum. The depth-optimal style strength is confounded with
semantic content (sky and distant background tend to co-occur at large depth; foreground
objects at small depth) and with the style image itself. A naive per-depth line search would
absorb content and style confounds into the depth map. The method must residualize the
covariate against coarse semantics and treat depth as one structured axis, not a free per-ray
parameter.

O2 -- Data poverty per depth band. Many scenes have few rays at the depth extremes (a thin
far band, or a near band occupying few pixels). Estimating an independent style strength per
depth band from such scarce, noisy evidence overfits. This forces hierarchical partial
pooling: data-poor bands must shrink toward the global default rather than chase noise.

O3 -- Covariate observability and leakage-freeness. The covariate must be computable from the
scene alone, without peeking at held-out views or at the style match. Rendered NeRF depth
qualifies (geometry only), but it is itself being optimized during fine-tuning; using a moving
target as a covariate can create feedback loops. The depth used to drive the map must be taken
from the frozen source (content) field and held fixed, and normalized across scenes for
comparability.

O4 -- Multi-axis heterogeneity. Depth is the primary axis, but the true optimal style field
also depends on view-grazing angle and local content. A single global scalar collapses all of
these; an unpooled per-ray field overfits all of them. The method needs a structured map that
captures the dominant depth axis while remaining extensible.

O5 -- Simultaneous lift, exact collapse, and a valid radiance field. The depth-weighting must
(i) improve the far-band/heterogeneous case, (ii) reduce exactly to the host method at its
default so there is provably no regression, and (iii) keep the fine-tuning objective
well-posed (the per-ray weights must not destabilize volumetric optimization or introduce
depth-correlated bias into geometry). Hitting all three at once is a tight constraint; methods
that only chase the lift routinely sacrifice the collapse guarantee.

O6 (BINDING) -- Metric blindness. The decisive obstacle is that the gap is invisible under the
field's pooled metrics (Section 2.3). A naive evaluation -- aggregate style score plus
single-view PSNR/LPIPS -- will report that the global method is already optimal and that any
depth-conditional variant is "within noise", because the depth-band-localized, opposite-signed
errors and the depth-localized flicker cancel in the means. Without depth-stratified
multi-view-consistency metrics and the right falsifiers, neither the problem nor the
improvement can be demonstrated at all. This is why straightforward approaches fail: they
optimize and measure the very quantity that hides the effect.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior work clusters into six near-miss families; each fails at least two of the four wedge
clauses that define the proposed method:

  C1 (operate only at the NeRF style-loss / supervision-weighting layer, reusing
     already-rendered depth and already-prepared style images, with zero added render,
     diffusion, or network cost);
  C2 (condition on an observable, leakage-free per-ray covariate -- rendered depth);
  C3 (use a structured, monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map -- not a single global
     scalar, and not unpooled free per-ray weights);
  C4 (provably collapse exactly to the global-strength host method at its default).

NM1 -- The source SWD-supervised NeRF and its SWD/Gram texture objective [20, 21, 22] (the
primary collapse target). Operates at the style-loss layer (C1) but uses one global style
strength: misses C2 and C3 (and is the C4 target).

NM2 -- Feature-matching radiance-field stylizers, ARF [11] and zero-shot StyleRF [12].
They match deep features in the field but, to our knowledge, apply a single global,
depth-unconditioned style objective over the whole field: miss C2, C3, and C4.

NM3 -- Instruction-driven 3D editing, Instruct-NeRF2NeRF [9] and ViCA-NeRF [10] with
InstructPix2Pix [16]. They use a single global edit strength and, in ViCA, explicit
correspondence; ViCA targets consistency but not via a depth-conditional style-strength map
with a collapse identity: miss C2, C3, and C4.

NM4 -- Feature-space and text-driven 3D stylization, SNeRF [13], NeRF-Art [14], Ref-NPR [15],
CLIP/CLIPstyler-style guidance [25, 26]. To our knowledge a single global, depth-unconditioned
style code/transform is applied to the whole field: miss C2, C3, and C4.

NM5 (SHARPEST) -- Depth-aware 2D neural style transfer [27, 28], including Kitov et al. [27]
which explicitly varies stylization strength by estimated depth. This family does condition
style on depth (the spirit of C2), which is why it is the sharpest near-miss and must be
delineated carefully. But it operates on single 2D images with no radiance field and no
multi-view notion (misses C1), uses a heuristic depth-loss/strength schedule rather than a
partially-pooled monotone map fit with empirical Bayes (misses C3), and offers no
exact-collapse identity to a 3D host (misses C4). Decisively, none of it addresses the
world-space-scale / multi-view-consistency mechanism that is the entire point in 3D: in 2D
there is only one view, so depth-graded strength is a layout-preservation trick, not a
cross-view consistency guarantee. The proposed method ports the depth-to-strength idea out of
the single image and into the radiance field, where it acquires a 3D-consistency meaning, a
partially-pooled estimator, and a safety identity.

NM6 -- Depth-conditioned generation, ControlNet [18] depth control on latent diffusion [19]
inside the style-aligned front end [17]. This conditions the generated content on depth, not
the style-transfer strength, and it lives in the diffusion stage, not the NeRF style loss; it
has no collapse identity to the NeRF objective: misses C1, C3, and C4.

No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4.

The barriers that kept this unsolved are concrete. First, in this very pipeline depth was
already present but used for the wrong job: it conditioned the diffusion model's content (a
ControlNet structural signal) and was rendered as an output, but it was never repurposed as
the covariate that selects style STRENGTH/SCALE inside the NeRF loss -- the signal was in hand
and used elsewhere. Second, the one community that did couple depth to style strength stayed in
2D [27, 28], where the multi-view consistency payoff (the main prize in 3D) does not exist, so
it had no reason to build the radiance-field machinery or the collapse identity. Third, pooled
whole-image metrics hid the depth-band misallocation and the far-band flicker (Section 2.3), so
there was no measured pressure to fix it. Fourth, the triple constraint of O5 -- far-band lift
AND flat-scene no-regression AND exact collapse -- was never co-targeted; methods optimized the
lift and discarded the safety identity. The proposed approach differs by treating rendered
depth as a first-class style-selection covariate, by enforcing exact collapse by construction,
and by introducing the depth-stratified consistency metric that makes the effect measurable.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method: STRATA (depth-Stratified, parTially-pooled style-stRength ATtenuation/Amplification).
STRATA promotes the global style strength lambda to a per-ray depth-conditional strength and,
optionally, promotes the global style scale to a depth-compensated scale, while reducing
exactly to the host at its default.

Covariate. For each ray r, take the normalized rendered depth zhat_r in [0,1] from the FROZEN
source (content) NeRF, computed as the standard volumetric expected ray-termination depth and
normalized by robust scene near/far percentiles for cross-scene comparability. Depth is
geometry-only, already computed by the renderer, already consumed as a structural control by
the depth-conditioned style-aligned diffusion front end [17, 18], and frozen before any style
gradient flows (resolving O3's feedback risk).

Primary knob K1 -- depth-conditional style strength. Replace the global lambda with

    w(zhat_r) = lambda_global x exp( g_theta(zhat_r - z0) ),  clamped to [w_min, w_max],

where z0 is the scene's median normalized depth (the anchor), u = zhat_r - z0 is centered
depth, and g_theta is an ANCHORED monotone map g_theta(u) = - sum_k theta_k (phi_k(u) -
phi_k(0)) built from a fixed dictionary of monotone non-decreasing basis functions phi_k with
non-negative coefficients theta_k >= 0. Subtracting phi_k(0) anchors g_theta(0) = 0, and the
explicit minus sign makes g_theta monotone NON-INCREASING in depth, so style strength DECREASES
with depth by default (near-emphasis), consistent with aerial perspective. Because every term
carries a learnable coefficient theta_k and no constant offset survives, theta = 0 makes
g_theta IDENTICALLY zero for all depths (not merely zero at the anchor). The reversed-direction
variant flips the sign (+ sum_k ...) and a non-monotone variant frees the basis; both are run as
ablation A6, so the monotone direction is a TESTED inductive bias, not an assumed truth. Below
the anchor the map amplifies strength (near surfaces) and above it attenuates (far surfaces);
centering at the median keeps the average multiplicative adjustment near one. Applying w to the SWD requires care, because SWD is a coupled distribution distance (it sorts
projected features globally), not a separable per-pixel loss; a sum of independent per-stratum
SWDs would NOT reduce to the host SWD and would break collapse. STRATA therefore keeps the SWD
over the SAME single global set of rendered features but replaces the uniform empirical measure
with a WEIGHTED empirical measure whose mixture weights are the NORMALIZED ray weights
alpha_r = w(zhat_r) / sum_r w(zhat_r). Concretely the style term is
L_swd_STRATA = ( mean_r w(zhat_r) ) x SWD( P_render(alpha), P_style ), where P_render(alpha) is
the rendered-feature distribution under the normalized weights alpha, SWD uses the standard
weighted-CDF / weighted-quantile matching, and the separable per-ray photometric term against
the prepared stylized image is scaled by w(zhat_r). At theta = 0, w(zhat_r) = lambda_global for
all r, so alpha is uniform (P_render(alpha) is the unweighted rendered distribution) and the
prefactor mean_r w(zhat_r) = lambda_global, giving L_swd_STRATA = lambda_global x SWD_host
exactly and the per-ray term = lambda_global x the host term; this
constant-weight identity is the exact-collapse hook below.

Optional knob K2 -- depth-compensated style scale (the consistency mechanism). Set the
image-space style scale s_img(zhat_r) = s0 x exp( h_psi(zhat_r - z0) ) with the default
schedule chosen so that s_img x z is held approximately constant in world space (h_psi pushes
toward s_img proportional to 1/z), realized by selecting the VGG layer / SWD slice
receptive-field scale (or the style-image sampling resolution) per depth stratum. psi = 0
recovers a single global scale s0. K2 is the knob that targets the scale mechanism directly:
holding the world-space texture scale approximately constant makes the imposed style project
consistently across views (exactly so under a pinhole projection of a fronto-parallel patch; up
to surface orientation and view angle in general). The scale-consistency thesis is therefore
tested through K2 (baseline B10), while K1 alone (B9) tests the strength / aerial-perspective
response; the two are reported separately so neither claim rides on the other.

Partial pooling. The deployable DEFAULT is a single shared map (one theta, and one psi if K2
is enabled) fit across the scene; this default is a single global map and is NOT itself claimed
as partially pooled. In empirical-Bayes terms the single shared map is the FULLY-pooled limit of
the hierarchical model below and the unpooled free per-ray weights are the NO-pooling limit;
STRATA's structured map is the partially-pooled estimator that interpolates between them, and the
shipped default sits at the fully-pooled end. The OPTIONAL refinement adds per-stratum offsets delta_k to the shared map
across K = 5 depth strata under a hierarchical empirical-Bayes model [30, 31]: a Gaussian prior
delta_k ~ N(0, tau^2) shrinking each stratum toward the shared map, a likelihood given by the
stratum's style-objective improvement, and tau estimated by marginal maximum likelihood, so the
posterior offset is a precision-weighted shrinkage that pulls data-poor strata (O2) back to the
default. The entire estimator -- normalization, strata, pooling, clamps, theta, psi, tau -- is
fit on the source field and the prepared style images only, and frozen before held-out views
are rendered.

Exact collapse (verifiable by construction). At theta = 0 (and psi = 0 if K2 is enabled) with
the clamp inactive (w_min < lambda_global < w_max), w(zhat_r) = lambda_global and s_img = s0 for
every ray, so the weighted empirical measure is uniform and the STRATA objective equals the
host's global SWD objective exactly; under identical random projections, minibatches, seeds,
feature normalization, and floating-point path the NeRF receives an identical gradient and the
optimization trajectory is identical update-for-update. The guard is computed on the global /
minibatch objective, not per ray: E_collapse = | L_STRATA - L_base | <= 1e-6, augmented by a
gradient check || grad L_STRATA - grad L_base ||_inf <= 1e-6, asserted in continuous
integration; the theta = 0 code path may also dispatch literally to the host loss. Nested
recovery: setting lambda_global to the source method's tuned global strength recovers the source
SWD-supervised NeRF exactly. Collapse and the isotropic null are DISTINCT claims. The theta = 0
identity guard holds on ANY scene. Separately, because the map is anchored at the scene median
z0, an exactly flat scene (all zhat_r equal, hence equal to their own median z0) has
zhat_r - z0 = 0 and w = lambda_global for ANY theta, so flat scenes collapse trivially; the
isotropic-null falsifier therefore probes LOW-depth-range but non-degenerate control scenes,
where STRATA can apply a small but real depth gradient and must still not beat a retuned global
baseline. If the robust near/far percentiles coincide (degenerate depth), normalization falls
back to the identity map (w = lambda_global).

Backbone and hosts. Backbone is nerfacto in Nerfstudio [2] (as in the source). Host H1 is the
source SWD-supervised NeRF (primary collapse target / identity guard). Host H2 is ARF's
nearest-neighbor feature-matching radiance field [11], to show host-agnostic generality; both
render depth, so C1 holds for both. A Gaussian-Splatting [7] port is named as future work.

Datasets. (1) Large-depth-range real captures: Mip-NeRF 360 unbounded outdoor scenes [3] and
Tanks-and-Temples-style outdoor captures -- the primary test, maximal depth heterogeneity.
(2) Forward-facing LLFF captures [8] -- moderate depth range. (3) A low-depth-range, near-planar
but non-degenerate control set -- the isotropic-null regime where STRATA can still apply a small
depth gradient yet must NOT beat a retuned global baseline B5, plus exactly-flat scenes as a
trivial-collapse sanity check. (4) Semi-synthetic Blender scenes with programmatically controlled
depth range and a known per-depth target strength -- ground truth for the monotone-dose test and
for strength-recovery error. A bank of text style prompts spanning painterly, sketch, and
material restyles is applied across all scenes.

Baselines. B1 content NeRF (no style); B2 source global-SWD method (PRIMARY collapse target /
identity guard); B3 ARF NNFM [11]; B4 Instruct-NeRF2NeRF [9] (instruction-line context); B5
RETUNED best-global style strength swept per scene (for the isotropic-null falsifier; distinct
from the theta = 0 identity guard); B6 unpooled free per-ray/per-band weights (overfit
control); B7 covariate-scrambled depth -- depth values permuted across rays (placebo); B8
per-depth-band oracle strength chosen with held-out access (impractical upper bound); B9 STRATA
(K1 only); B10 STRATA (K1 + K2).

Metrics. PRIMARY: depth-stratified multi-view consistency error. For each of a fixed set of
view pairs (adjacent and medium-baseline, sampled from held-out poses), warp the stylized render
of view i into view j using the FROZEN content field's rendered depth and the known camera
poses; mask occlusions/disocclusions with a forward-backward depth/flow consistency test
(cross-checked with optical flow in the spirit of blind video temporal consistency [29]); assign
each surviving pixel to one of K depth bands by its warped rendered depth (band boundaries =
depth quantiles of the scene); and report the masked photometric + LPIPS-feature warp error
averaged within each band, with the FAR band as the headline, alongside far-band stylization
quality. SECONDARY: whole-scene style match (SWD/Gram/CLIP-style distance)
held at no-regression versus B2 within a pre-registered margin; content/structure preservation
(LPIPS and rendered-depth error to the content field); single-view perceptual quality; a small
user study on perceived 3D consistency and style fidelity; depth calibration of the learned map;
and E_collapse <= 1e-6. Reporting depth-stratified-and-then-aggregated quantities is essential:
pooled-only numbers would reproduce the metric blindness of O6.

Ablations. A1 covariate (rendered depth vs scrambled vs a semantic-content proxy); A2 monotone
basis family and count; A3 number of depth strata K; A4 pooling strength (single global map vs
empirical-Bayes vs unpooled); A5 clamp band [w_min, w_max] and clamp-hit fraction; A6 map
DIRECTION (near-emphasis vs far-emphasis vs non-monotone); A7 K2 scale knob on/off and the
world-space-constant schedule.

Falsifiers (each can refute the mechanism). T1 isotropic-null: on the low-depth-range
non-degenerate control set, STRATA must NOT outperform the RETUNED best-global baseline B5 (if
it does, the gain is not coming from depth); this is deliberately separate from the theta = 0
exact-identity guard, which holds on every scene.
T2 monotone-dose: on semi-synthetic scenes the consistency/quality gain must grow with
programmed depth range and shrink to zero as depth range -> 0. T3 covariate-scramble: replacing
true depth with permuted depth (B7) must destroy the gain.

Hypotheses (numeric ranges are conjectured, with no preliminary results). H1: on large-depth
-range outdoor scenes, STRATA reduces far-band multi-view inconsistency by roughly 20-45%
relative to B2 with no whole-scene style-match regression beyond the pre-registered margin. H2:
the gain grows monotonically with scene depth range (T2 holds). H3: on low-depth-range
non-degenerate control scenes STRATA matches the retuned global B5 within noise (T1 holds), and
exactly-flat scenes collapse trivially. H4: covariate-scramble erases at least 80% of the gain (T3
holds). H5: K2 (world-space-constant scale) contributes a measurable additional far-band
consistency improvement over K1 alone. H6: E_collapse <= 1e-6 and nested recovery of the source
method hold by construction (verifiable, not merely conjectured).

Expected outcomes (made clear). STRATA is expected to deliver visibly more multi-view-consistent
stylization on exactly the large-depth-range outdoor scenes that the source method showcases,
to leave flat scenes and whole-scene style fidelity essentially unchanged, to expose -- via the
new depth-stratified consistency metric -- a structured far-band failure of global methods that
pooled metrics hide, and to do all of this as a drop-in layer that provably reduces to its host
at the default, so it can be adopted with no regression at the exact-collapse setting (active
depth-conditional configurations are validation-gated).

Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations (Rai folds). F1 -- Content provenance and
licensing: the diffusion front end [17, 18, 19] and any artistic style reference carry
license/consent obligations; respect generative-model usage licenses, avoid imitating a living
artist's signature style without consent, and document style-image sources. F2 -- Sensitive
content and fairness: scenes containing people or culturally significant sites must not be
degraded or caricatured by depth-graded stylization; report quality disaggregated by scene
category, and ensure depth is not used as a proxy to systematically suppress detail on any
sensitive region. F3 -- Do-no-harm default: the theta = 0 (and psi = 0) exact-collapse setting
is the shipped default; active depth-conditional configurations are validation-gated and
shadow-deployable, always recoverable to the host. F4 -- Scientific integrity: all numeric
hypotheses are marked conjectured with no preliminary results; the E_collapse unit test, the
semi-synthetic depth-controlled generators, seeds, and the depth-stratified consistency metric
are released for reproduction. F5 -- Governance and auditability: the learned map w(zhat) (and
s_img(zhat)), the depth estimator, the strata boundaries, the clamp band, and the pooling
strength are all logged and auditable per scene, with human review for high-stakes or
people-containing captures before any active configuration ships.
